title: Support of named volumes in podman-build/buildah-bud
body:
**Description**

I would like the ability to use named data volumes in podman-build/buildah-bud.

Maybe my use case is too special to warrent the work, but I figured I might as well ask.


We build images in two steps, using a host dir for data exchange:

Step one makes the actual build, and copies the relevant output to the shared volume.

Step two prepares the resulting runtime image, using data from the volume.



This could normally be done in one file, without the need to exchange data between two podman-build executions.

But we also need to add build-process-computed labels to the end-image.
And there is (to my knowledge) no way to do that in the Containerfile syntax.

So part of the output from step 1 is appended to the Containerfile used in step 2.
And thus allows us to use build-computed LABEL statements when creating the end-image.



Using a named image would avoid the problem of cleaning on the host file system after the build.

The containers run with whatever uid they are designed to use, and that maps to uid+9999 on the host filesystem.
These files are a hassle to delete afterwards.

The build process cannot have root permissions, so the current solution is to generate a Containerfile with suitable cleanup commands and run it as step 3.



I hope the above makes some sense?

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Could you give an simple example of a Containerfile and a buildah command you would like to see.

A rather banal example:

* Containerfile.producer

```
FROM alpine

VOLUME /output

RUN echo "Computed information" > /output/data.txt
```

* Containerfile.consumer

```
FROM alpine

VOLUME /input

# Use data file from producer
RUN cp /input/data.txt /
```


Without volume support, it goes something like:

```
podman build -v /hostpath/exchange:/output:Z -f Containerfile.producer

podman build -v /hostpath/exchange:/input:Z,ro -f Containerfile.consumer


# dynamically construct Containerfile.cleanup
podman build -v /hostpath/exchange:/input:Z -f Containerfile.cleanup

```

We run builds on OCP without admin (but with setuid), so cleanup of /hostpath/exchange is painful.

Depending on what user ids are in play in the producer container, /hostpath/exchange may end up containing files that the user running podman cannot delete.

So we construct a new Containerfile.cleanup with suitable USER/RUN commands to clean up.




If podman build could take volumes, cleanup would be as simple as:

```
podman volume create exchange

podman build -v exchange:/output:Z -f Containerfile.producer

podman build -v exchange:/input:Z -f Containerfile.consumer

podman volume rm exchange
```


Again, this may be a little too outré to be worth pursuing :)

@mheon we would need a way to create and mount a named volume.  Then modify the volumes source to point at the mountpoint of the named volume. When the build completes it would need to unmount the named volume. WDYT?

Do we need these to be Podman named volumes?

We could add Mount/Unmount APIs to Libpod's named volume API, but then we create the kind of circular dependency issues between Libpod and Buildah that we were trying to avoid. If we can't do that, I think you're looking at an extensive rewrite, as the Libpod code was never written to be portable and really isn't.

Well I guess it would be best to tell user to just create a directory and use volume mounts for this, I don't think it is worth the effort.
Sorry @jskovjyskebankdk But this is not going to be easy to implement.  And the workaround should be fairly easy for you.


mkdir /tmp/exchange; chcon -t container_file_t /tmp/exchange

podman build -v /tmp/exchange:/output -f Containerfile.producer

podman build -v /tmp/exchange:/input -f Containerfile.consumer

rm -rf /tmp/exchange

No problem.

I will try the chcon trick.

Thanks for the consideration.

Cheers!
